exercises.example_sentence: 雨が降っている__、傘を持って行きます。
exercises.translation: Because it's raining, I'll take an umbrella.

wrong_choice_explanations.ので: ので also means 'because' but is more formal — から is more appropriate for this simple, direct reason.
wrong_choice_explanations.のに: のに means 'even though' or 'despite' — it expresses contrast, not causation.
wrong_choice_explanations.けれど: けれど means 'but' or 'however' — it shows contrast, not the causal relationship needed here.

exercises.example_sentence: 時間がない__、急いでください。
exercises.translation: Because we don't have time, please hurry.

wrong_choice_explanations.ので: ので also means 'because' but is more formal — から is more direct for urgent situations.
wrong_choice_explanations.でも: でも means 'but' or 'however' — it shows contrast, not the reason for hurrying.
wrong_choice_explanations.と: と is a conditional 'if/when' — it doesn't express the causal reason for the request.

exercises.translation: Because I was tired, I went to bed early.

wrong_choice_explanations.ので: ので can also mean 'because' but is more formal — から is more natural for personal experiences.
wrong_choice_explanations.のに: のに means 'even though' — this would contradict the logical connection between being tired and sleeping.
wrong_choice_explanations.が: が can connect clauses but usually shows contrast — it doesn't clearly express the causal relationship.

exercises.example_sentence: お金がない__、買えません。
exercises.translation: Because I don't have money, I can't buy it.

wrong_choice_explanations.ので: ので also means 'because' but is more formal — から is more straightforward for simple explanations.
wrong_choice_explanations.でも: でも means 'but' — it would create contradiction instead of explaining the reason for not buying.
wrong_choice_explanations.し: し can list reasons but is less direct — から more clearly shows the single cause-effect relationship.

exercises.example_sentence: 明日は休み__、ゆっくりできます。
exercises.translation: Because tomorrow is a holiday, I can relax.

wrong_choice_explanations.なので: なので is more formal and typically follows nouns with な — だから is more natural here.
wrong_choice_explanations.だけど: だけど means 'but' — it shows contrast, not the reason for being able to relax.
wrong_choice_explanations.でも: でも means 'but' or 'however' — it doesn't express the causal relationship between holiday and relaxation.

exercises.example_sentence: 道が分からない__、地図を見ています。
exercises.translation: Because I don't know the way, I'm looking at a map.

wrong_choice_explanations.ので: ので can also mean 'because' but is more formal — から is more direct for immediate situations.
wrong_choice_explanations.のに: のに means 'even though' — this would create an illogical contrast.
wrong_choice_explanations.けど: けど means 'but' — it shows contrast, not the reason for looking at the map.

exercises.example_sentence: 頭が痛い__、薬を飲みました。
exercises.translation: Because my head hurt, I took medicine.

wrong_choice_explanations.ので: ので can also mean 'because' but is more formal — から is more natural for personal health situations.
wrong_choice_explanations.のに: のに means 'even though' — this would create contradiction instead of explaining the reason.
wrong_choice_explanations.と: と as a conditional means 'when/if' — it doesn't express the past causal relationship.

exercises.example_sentence: 暑い__、窓を開けてください。
exercises.translation: Because it's hot, please open the window.

wrong_choice_explanations.ので: ので can mean 'because' but is more formal — から is more direct for requests.
wrong_choice_explanations.でも: でも means 'but' — it doesn't provide the reason for the request.
wrong_choice_explanations.けど: けど means 'but' — it shows contrast, not the reason for opening the window.

exercises.example_sentence: 今日は忙しい__、会えません。
exercises.translation: Because I'm busy today, I can't meet.

wrong_choice_explanations.ので: ので can also mean 'because' but is more formal — から is more direct for declining invitations.
wrong_choice_explanations.のに: のに means 'even though' — this would create illogical contrast.
wrong_choice_explanations.が: が can connect clauses but often shows contrast — it doesn't clearly express the causal reason.

exercises.example_sentence: 電車が遅れた__、遅刻しました。
exercises.translation: Because the train was delayed, I was late.

wrong_choice_explanations.ので: ので can also mean 'because' but is more formal — から is more straightforward for explaining lateness.
wrong_choice_explanations.のに: のに means 'even though' — this would create contradiction instead of explaining the cause.
wrong_choice_explanations.でも: でも means 'but' — it doesn't express the causal relationship between the delay and being late.
